title: Sad Sack

jokes.text: Narrator: 'It's a new prosecutor. It's a whole new beginning. A new day, a new case, a whole new set of lies.' / Michael: 'I'm not lying anymore, Mom.' / Narrator: 'Michael was lying.'
jokes.setup: Lucille cheerfully frames the new prosecutor as 'a whole new set of lies,' while Michael insists he's no longer lying.
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately and flatly confirms: 'Michael was lying.'
jokes.explanation: A perfect example of the show's narrator-as-comedian technique. The timing is impeccable — Michael barely finishes his denial before the narrator undercuts it. The brevity ('Michael was lying.') is the whole joke. High character integration because Michael's self-deception is core to his arc. Scores an 8+ because it's among the sharpest deployments of the show's signature structural device.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'I'm not lying anymore either, Michael. I'm being lied... on.'
jokes.setup: Michael says he's not lying anymore; Lucille mirrors the statement.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm being lied... on' — a grammatically tortured construction that means nothing but sounds accusatory.
jokes.explanation: A brilliant piece of character writing. Lucille takes Michael's phrasing, refuses to cede the moral high ground, and invents a nonsense locution that perfectly captures her victim mentality and linguistic laziness. 'Being lied on' is a phrase that makes no sense but is delivered with complete conviction — pure Lucille. Highly quotable and character-specific.

jokes.text: Narrator, after Lucille eats the egg whites George Sr. was waiting for: 'Wow, now more is going to come up than go down.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is starving in the attic waiting for breakfast; Lucille eats the egg whites.
jokes.punchline: Narrator's offhand remark implies Lucille will make herself vomit, delivered with absurd casualness.
jokes.explanation: Dark, efficient, and perfectly in the narrator's deadpan register. The implication of bulimia is handled so lightly it lands as wit rather than shock. Character-specific (Lucille's vanity and disordered eating are recurring traits) and economical.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'This is the nicest she's been since she found out that Rosa could breast-feed Buster.'
jokes.setup: Oscar is complaining about Lucille being too affectionate.
jokes.punchline: Lucille casually references that their housekeeper Rosa breast-fed the adult Buster as a benchmark of Lucille's past happiness — treating it as a completely normal data point.
jokes.explanation: Multiple layers: (1) the absurdity of an adult being breast-fed, (2) Lucille's delight that she didn't have to nurture her own child, (3) Buster's pathological dependence. The casualness of delivery is everything. High character integration — only Lucille would say this, and only Buster would have this backstory.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Aren't you supposed to yell at me and call me "homo" and motivate me over this wall?' / Drill Sergeant: 'We can't do that anymore. Lawsuit.'
jokes.setup: Buster is failing to climb over the training wall and asks the sergeant to motivate him the old-fashioned way.
jokes.punchline: The sergeant confirms this was normal procedure but is now legally prohibited.
jokes.explanation: 'Lawsuit.' as a one-word response is beautifully economical. The joke works on multiple levels: satirizing military culture, Buster's naivety in requesting verbal abuse, and the absurdity that this was standard practice. Good economy pulls the score up.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'In fact, it was a recent ruling, prosecuted by the same man who Michael was soon to meet with. It's going to be a long time before Sergeant Wendell Baker calls someone "Private Homo" again.'
jokes.setup: The lawsuit preventing homophobic insults at boot camp.
jokes.punchline: The prosecutor who will feature in the main plot also prosecuted this military harassment case — a planted connection that pays off later.
jokes.explanation: A masterful piece of foreshadowing-as-comedy. Connecting the B-plot military harassment lawsuit to the A-plot new prosecutor plants a connection viewers won't catch on first watch. The specificity of 'Private Homo' and the sergeant's name add texture. High rewatch bonus — funnier retroactively.

jokes.text: George Sr. on attic floor with breast pump: 'God willing, he'll fail boot camp. Well, why wouldn't he? He already failed day camp.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is discussing Buster's military career from the attic.
jokes.punchline: He has no faith in Buster, culminating in the revelation that Buster failed day camp — a lower-stakes precursor that makes his Army failure inevitable.
jokes.explanation: The escalating failure from day camp to Army is a perfect comedic regression. 'Day camp' specifically is funnier than 'summer camp' — the most benign, low-stakes environment imaginable. George Sr.'s casual dismissal of his son is deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: Michael tells George-Michael that Ann got him a B-minus. George-Michael: 'Well, it wasn't Ann. She's an expert in math.' Michael: 'Isn't that cute?' / Narrator: 'Is it?'
jokes.setup: George-Michael defends Ann's math expertise with genuine admiration.
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'Isn't that cute?' is dripping with condescension; the narrator's 'Is it?' pulls the rug further — the show's ongoing joke of Ann being unimpressive.
jokes.explanation: Part of the show's sustained 'Her?' gag about Ann. The narrator joining Michael in passive skepticism is a nice doubling. Functions well as a running gag beat even if this particular iteration isn't peak-Ann-joke.

jokes.text: Michael suggests George-Michael needs glasses for grades, not eyesight: 'Maybe it's your eyes. Maybe you need glasses. For your grades.'
jokes.setup: Michael doesn't want to directly criticize Ann, so he invents a medical pretext.
jokes.punchline: 'For your grades.' — the clarification makes the fabrication explicit and funnier.
jokes.explanation: Funny in isolation, but its real value is as setup for a later payoff (the glasses gag). The rewatch bonus comes from knowing this planted detail will return. Michael's manipulative parenting is character-specific.

jokes.text: Tobias enters dramatically, claims he's 'afraid he'll have to take 'em to go' because he's 'late for the gym.' Lindsay responds by handing him the Blue Man Group understudy number.
jokes.setup: Tobias shows up for eggs but announces he's off to the gym.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay has apparently already arranged an understudy hotline for Tobias's Blue Man Group obsession — the running gag of Tobias's failed acting career treated as routine household logistics.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the casual integration of Tobias's ridiculous dream into daily family logistics. 'Here's the number in case they call' implies Lindsay keeps it on hand — a funny background detail about the absurdity of the household.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'I just found out that my cellular telephone was a lemon. It didn't work.' Lindsay: 'Coincidentally, neither do you.'
jokes.setup: Tobias complains his cell phone was defective.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay delivers a caustic parallel: Tobias himself is equally non-functional — a devastating spousal burn.
jokes.explanation: Sharp, economical, and well-earned. Lindsay's wit is underused in the show, so when she lands one, it's satisfying. The parallel construction (lemon phone = lemon husband) is elegant, and 'neither do you' is delivered with three words that do the work of a whole speech.

jokes.text: Lindsay's raspy, hoarse voice. Michael: 'What's wrong with your voice?' Lindsay: 'Nothing. I've been going out.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay sounds noticeably different — hoarse from her late nights at singles clubs.
jokes.punchline: 'Nothing. I've been going out.' — the non-answer that is also a complete answer, delivered without self-awareness.
jokes.explanation: A compact characterization joke. Lindsay's inability to see her own behavior as problematic is perfectly captured. The voice detail presumably plays as a visual/audio gag; the explanation is funny in its inadequacy.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Lindsay had been frequenting singles clubs, also still hoping to make her open marriage work... [Lindsay: 'Whoo!'] ...which it won't.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is clubbing hoping to save her open marriage.
jokes.punchline: '...which it won't.' — the narrator's flat dismissal mid-sentence, punctuated by Lindsay's oblivious 'Whoo!'
jokes.explanation: The narrator's interjection of '...which it won't.' after cutting to footage of Lindsay's delusion is structurally brilliant. The placement between two Lindsay 'Whoo!'s makes it funnier — she can't hear her own story being undercut. Economy is near-perfect: three words of narration do enormous work.

jokes.text: Michael: 'That's why they call it "scoring."' Lindsay: 'Maybe in the '70s.' Lindsay: 'That many?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay says competing with Tobias is like 'scoring'; Michael dismisses this as dated slang.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay misinterprets Michael's '70s' reference as a number of sexual partners — a miscommunication that reveals her fixation.
jokes.explanation: A clean double-meaning joke where the miscommunication is organic rather than forced. '70s' as decade vs. number works because both readings are plausible, and Lindsay's 'That many?' reveals her own competitive mindset without a single wasted word.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Instead of competing with Tobias, why don't you try spending more time with your daughter?' Lindsay: 'Why? Tobias doing that?'
jokes.setup: Michael suggests Lindsay be a better mother.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's only frame of reference is competitive — she'll parent only if Tobias is parenting. The joke reveals she has no intrinsic motivation to be a mother.
jokes.explanation: Four words — 'Why? Tobias doing that?' — that perfectly encapsulate Lindsay's character: competitive, avoidant, and completely lacking in maternal instinct. Could only be said by this character. Near-perfect economy. The timing implicit in the rhythm of the exchange is excellent.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'At that moment, Lindsay's daughter Maeby was in the process of wooing her own man.' / Maeby's delivery boy arrives. 'Who is it?' / 'Steve Holt!'
jokes.setup: Steve Holt announces himself as a delivery boy.
jokes.punchline: Steve Holt always shouts his own name — a character tic that's both absurd and endearing. Here it works as both introduction and gag.
jokes.explanation: The Steve Holt name-shout gag is a running bit that accumulates value. Each iteration is funny partly due to the callback and partly because the character's self-announcement is so gloriously unnecessary. High quotability because fans repeat 'Steve Holt!' reflexively.

jokes.text: Steve Holt on Maeby: 'Is this your sister?' Maeby: 'No...' Steve: 'But you just made my day.' / Maeby orders 'Memories of Thanksgiving' basket just to see Steve.
jokes.setup: Steve thinks Maeby might have a cute sister, which she doesn't.
jokes.punchline: His 'you just made my day' suggests Maeby herself is the cute one — but the narrator already told us Maeby is ordering unnecessary items just to create delivery occasions, making her scheming simultaneously validated and undercut.
jokes.explanation: A competent character moment. The 'Memories of Thanksgiving' basket detail is a nice absurdist touch (who orders this?) but the exchange itself is fairly standard teen-comedy fare elevated by the show's density.

jokes.text: George-Michael about Steve Holt: 'He's 19. He's a senior at my high school.' Narrator: 'And had been for several years.'
jokes.setup: George-Michael presents Steve Holt's age/grade as disqualifying.
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals Steve has been a senior multiple years — he's been held back repeatedly, adding pathetic texture to his confident name-announcement.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's addition ('And had been for several years') is perfectly deadpan and recontextualizes Steve Holt entirely. The brevity is ideal — five words that replace what could have been an entire scene of exposition.

jokes.text: Lindsay (after the Steve Holt moment): 'You know what? I think I am going to spend more time with my daughter.' Michael: 'I can't promise you I won't tell the prosecutor about that.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay announces she'll be a better mother — after her own flirtatious exchange with the teenage delivery boy.
jokes.punchline: Michael's threat to tell the prosecutor about Lindsay's behavior toward a teenager is darkly funny, revealing the actual reason for her renewed maternal interest.
jokes.explanation: Michael's prosecutor threat is a funny overcorrection that also grounds the scene in the episode's A-plot. The joke works because we know Lindsay's 'mothering' is itself inappropriate, making Michael's legal threat weirdly proportionate.

jokes.text: Michael finds George Sr. in attic wearing his dead wife's maternity clothes.
jokes.setup: Michael goes to the attic to check on his fugitive father.
jokes.punchline: George Sr. is wearing Tracy's maternity clothes — the only thing he could find. The visual of a heavyset man in maternity wear is absurd; that they belonged to Michael's deceased mother adds dark texture.
jokes.explanation: A strong visual gag that implies rather than explains. 'Are you wearing my dead wife's maternity clothes?' as a sentence is absurd on its face. George Sr.'s explanation ('It's the only thing I could find') is perfectly inadequate. The dark tinge (deceased wife's clothes) keeps it complex.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Where are the eggs?' George Sr.: 'Doctor says they're bad for the baby.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is wearing maternity clothes and has been to a doctor.
jokes.punchline: He's apparently committed to the bit — referencing 'the baby' as if the maternity costuming reflects reality, or more likely he has a doctor who doesn't know he's a fugitive man.
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the maternity clothes gag — now George Sr. is actually consulting a doctor as if pregnant. The commitment to the absurd premise is what makes it funny. 'Bad for the baby' lands well because of what we know.

jokes.text: George Sr. fumbles with a ham can: 'How do you get this ham open? I can... I can't get this ham open.' Then discovers it peels open: 'Oh. Oh, look, it just peels off. Isn't that clever?'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is simultaneously making dramatic accusations and fumbling with a canned ham.
jokes.punchline: 'Isn't that clever?' — genuine delight at discovering pull-tab packaging, mid-crisis conversation.
jokes.explanation: 'Isn't that clever?' is a perfect comedy beat — the contrast between George Sr.'s supposed criminal mastermind status and his delight at pull-tab packaging is rich. The line derails a serious family conversation with genuine absurdist joy. High character integration: a man who built atrocities finds a ham tin fascinating.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'Your mother happens to be just shtupping my brother, that's all. I'm only here to find out if I have a shot with her. If she's really in love with Oscar, I don't know, to hell with her — I'll just leave.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. explains his reason for coming back after faking his death.
jokes.punchline: His entire motivational calculus is purely jealousy over his twin brother — not his children, not his freedom, just romantic competition with Oscar.
jokes.explanation: The reductive explanation for George Sr.'s return (jealousy, not paternal love) is both funny and character-revealing. 'To hell with her — I'll just leave' treats his fugitive status as a minor inconvenience compared to romantic rejection.

jokes.text: George-Michael's eye exam: 'One or two?' 'Two. No, no, one. Okay, two...' / 'Or three?' 'Three. Unless three is too much of an improvement. I'm sorry, is one... is one in the mix still?'
jokes.setup: George-Michael undergoes an eye exam he doesn't need, having been manipulated into it.
jokes.punchline: He applies the same indecisive exam-taking strategy to vision testing that lost him the math test — 'Unless three is too much of an improvement' shows he's trying to fail on purpose so Ann isn't implicated.
jokes.explanation: This is excellent comedy writing because it mirrors the math exam joke structurally (overthinking, indecision, fifth choice) while adding a new layer: GM is trying not to outperform Ann's glasses prescription. 'Unless three is too much of an improvement' and 'is one in the mix still?' are brilliantly neurotic. The callback to Michael's earlier manipulation makes this funnier on rewatch. Scores 8+ for integrating character, callback, and absurdist escalation simultaneously.

jokes.text: Michael on Gob: 'He's twice tried to microwave a Ding-Dong while it was still in its foil... twice.' Wayne Jarvis: 'Twice?' Michael: 'Two times.'
jokes.setup: Michael is defending Gob's ignorance about the business by establishing his general incompetence.
jokes.punchline: '...twice.' then the clarification 'Two times.' — the redundant elaboration on an already-redundant original statement is the joke.
jokes.explanation: A masterfully constructed triple-redundancy joke: 'twice' appears in the original sentence, then again at the end, then Michael explains it as 'two times' when Jarvis questions it. The comedy lives in Michael's completely straight-faced insistence on reconfirming what is already self-evident. Very high quotability — fans of the show repeat 'Two times.' Often. Strong character integration: perfectly captures both Gob's idiocy and Michael's exasperation.

jokes.text: Interrogators: 'He knows less than anyone we have ever questioned.' Narrator: 'Gob knew too little to know that, however, and his confidence had never been more shaken.'
jokes.setup: Gob is told he's free to go after questioning — he apparently performed spectacularly badly.
jokes.punchline: The interrogators' professional assessment of Gob's ignorance is a compliment; Gob, not understanding this, is shaken. The narrator explains the irony with characteristically dry precision.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: (1) Gob's ignorance is so total it impresses intelligence professionals, and (2) his pride is wounded by the very exoneration that saves him. The narrator's framing ('knew too little to know that') is precise and funny. Classic Gob character comedy.

jokes.text: Wayne Jarvis: 'Things could get very messy for you, Michael, unless you have something to bargain with, like, say... a fugitive.' Michael: 'I don't know where to find such a thing.'
jokes.setup: Wayne Jarvis is clearly hinting that Michael should turn in his father.
jokes.punchline: Michael's response ('I don't know where to find such a thing') while his father is in the attic above is delivered with perfect comic insincerity.
jokes.explanation: The spatial irony (father in attic, Michael in meeting) makes this funnier than the dialogue alone. Michael's plausible deniability phrasing is slightly labored but serviceable.

jokes.text: Barry: 'Can I have a moment alone with my client, please?' [to Jarvis and aide] 'Did you follow any of that?' Michael then explains the obvious deal being offered. Barry: 'Do you know where your father is? No, wait, don't tell me now. Okay, if you know, give me a high five.'
jokes.setup: Barry is Michael's lawyer but apparently followed none of the prosecutor's hints.
jokes.punchline: 'If you know, give me a high five' — Barry's workaround for attorney-client privilege in the dumbest possible way: a high five as coded communication.
jokes.explanation: Barry Zuckerkorn is one of TV's great incompetent lawyers, and 'give me a high five' as a legal communication strategy is inspired. The sequence builds perfectly: Barry's complete failure to follow the meeting, his refusal to hear information directly, and his improvised workaround. Earns 8+ because the high five solution is both character-specific and an original comedic invention that feels earned from Barry's established incompetence.

jokes.text: Barry: 'Well, if he's got a shot with her, just give me a little tap on the fanny.'
jokes.setup: Michael explains George Sr.'s situation; Barry advises on how to communicate updates.
jokes.punchline: Barry continues his coded communication system — now a 'tap on the fanny' — which is both an unnecessary escalation and immediately suspicious.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'high five' to 'tap on the fanny' as a communication method is a funny character beat — Barry's obliviousness to the obvious is consistent and his physical communication vocabulary gets more inappropriate as he goes. The joke also plants the Barry-is-gay subtext the show returns to.

jokes.text: Barry: 'Just trust me. I'm a lot more competent than you can imagine.' Immediately forgets the tinfoil on his Ding-Dong: 'Oh, I forgot to take the tinfoil off, too. I really wanted that.'
jokes.setup: Barry asserts his competence.
jokes.punchline: He then laments the microwave-ruined Ding-Dong — confirming he makes the same mistake as Gob ('twice') and is equally incompetent.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's densest callback payoff. Barry microwaves a tinfoil-wrapped Ding-Dong IMMEDIATELY after asserting his competence — the same mistake Gob made twice, which was just cited as evidence of Gob's stupidity. Plus the prosecutor asks for a 'hot Ding Dong' in the next scene. Three jokes intersect: Barry's incompetence claim, the Gob callback, and the foreshadowing of the prosecutor line. The structure is nearly perfect, and 'I really wanted that.' is a devastatingly sad punchline. Earns its 8.6 craft score for multi-layered construction.

jokes.text: Gob motivating Buster to jump: 'Do it!' / Buster jumps onto Gob and punches him in the chest. Gob: 'Now, when you do this without getting punched in the chest, you'll have more fun.'
jokes.setup: Gob offers to help Buster practice jumping.
jokes.punchline: Buster's attempt immediately harms Gob; Gob's coaching advice adjusts for getting punched rather than addressing the actual problem.
jokes.explanation: Physical slapstick with a great button line. 'When you do this without getting punched in the chest, you'll have more fun' is a funny piece of coaching that implies this is just a minor variable to optimize rather than a fundamental problem. High rewatch bonus because this exact line is reused later in the episode.

jokes.text: Gob convinces Buster they should go to the 'old playground.' Tobias then appears asking to borrow Gob's phone. Gob: 'I'm not fit to run a company, and I don't deserve a fancy phone.' Tobias: 'Well, the Blue Man Group might need me, and I do deserve a fancy phone.' Narrator: 'They didn't, and he doesn't.'
jokes.setup: Tobias asks for the phone because the Blue Man Group might call.
jokes.punchline: The narrator's 'They didn't, and he doesn't.' — a two-part simultaneous deflation of both of Tobias's claims.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's four-word response ('They didn't, and he doesn't.') is economically perfect. It's a parallel structure that dismantles two claims simultaneously: (1) The Blue Man Group didn't call, (2) Tobias doesn't deserve a fancy phone. The rhythm is great, the character specificity is high, and the dismissal is total. One of the narrator's best lines in the episode.

jokes.text: The Blue Man Group might need me, and I do deserve a fancy phone. / [Narrator: They didn't, and he doesn't.]
jokes.setup: Tobias is trying to borrow Gob's phone because the Blue Man Group might call for an understudy.
jokes.punchline: The narrator delivers a two-part simultaneous refutation: they didn't call, and he doesn't deserve it.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's double refutation ('They didn't, and he doesn't') is structurally elegant — it addresses both the factual claim and the normative one with a comma and parallel construction. The Blue Man Group running gag is established series lore and works as a quick hit here.

jokes.text: Lucille to Oscar: 'And it's so nice not to have to worry about getting pregnant. The doctor said I couldn't be a mother now if I tried. And that was without even interviewing me.'
jokes.setup: Lucille is expressing her contentment with Oscar.
jokes.punchline: 'And that was without even interviewing me' — Lucille implies that if the doctor had assessed her personality (not just her biology), she would be even more unfit. She's perversely proud of this.
jokes.explanation: Among the episode's best lines. The joke requires understanding that Lucille is a terrible mother who knows it — and the dark kicker ('without even interviewing me') transforms what could be sad into triumphant self-awareness delivered without remorse. The structure is surgical: setup (can't get pregnant), false bottom (biological fact), then the real punchline (personality would have disqualified her anyway). Could only be said by Lucille. Earns 8.8 for originality, character integration, and dark wit working in perfect concert.

jokes.text: Oscar to Lucille: 'Here you go: hot tea. Because that's what you are: a hot-tie.'
jokes.setup: Oscar brings Lucille tea as a loving gesture.
jokes.punchline: 'Hot tea... hot-tie' — a pun so weak and earnest that it loops back to being charming. Oscar's dopey sincerity makes the bad wordplay work.
jokes.explanation: An intentionally terrible pun that works because Oscar is coded as the lovably dopey twin. The show uses Oscar's sincere awkwardness to get away with weak wordplay by making the badness of the joke the joke itself. Functional but not sharp.

jokes.text: Oscar to Michael: 'Stop me from pouring this on myself. I can't take this anymore.' [re: Lucille's affection]
jokes.setup: Oscar is being smothered by an unusually loving Lucille.
jokes.punchline: He's so overwhelmed he's considering self-scalding. The extremity of the reaction to kindness is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The callback to the opening setup (Lucille's affection is unbearable) lands but doesn't add much new. Competent extension of an established joke rather than a fresh angle.

jokes.text: Michael suggests Oscar should 'take a leap at freedom like my father' and 'run.' Oscar: 'Is that her rape horn?' Michael: 'It's more like a starter's pistol.' / Narrator: 'Run.'
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to chase Oscar away so his father doesn't leave; he encourages Oscar to 'run.'
jokes.punchline: The 'rape horn' / 'starter's pistol' exchange reframes the sound as race-starting rather than danger — then the narrator gives Oscar the one-word command: 'Run.' The narrator is now directing characters.
jokes.explanation: The 'rape horn' terminology is darkly absurd (implying Lucille used this device often enough to name it). 'Starter's pistol' reframes it beautifully. But the real punchline is the narrator breaking the fourth wall to give a direct one-word instruction — 'Run.' — which is both a direction to Oscar and the show's narrator momentarily becoming a character. A smart meta-joke with real economy.

jokes.text: Lindsay arrives at school to spend time with Maeby, meets Steve Holt. Lindsay: 'After all, hasn't been that long since I've been in high school.' Steve: 'Is that a fire?' Students: 'We have to go to class.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay's attempt at bonding with her teenage daughter involves hanging out with Maeby's crush.
jokes.punchline: Steve Holt's 'Is that a fire?' — his attempt to escape Lindsay's awkward oversharing — followed by everyone fleeing to class.
jokes.explanation: The cringe of Lindsay trying to seem young while her daughter watches is well-established territory for the character. Steve Holt's 'Is that a fire?' is a great panic-escape attempt. The scene functions well but doesn't break new comedic ground.

jokes.text: Lindsay to Steve and Maeby: 'Maybe later we can all spend a little more time together, go shopping, get a drink.' / Maeby: 'I'm in school, and I'm 15.' Lindsay: 'Oh, well, I'm much, much, much older than 15.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay suggests getting drinks with her teenage daughter's friends.
jokes.punchline: Her clarification that she is 'much, much, much older than 15' is both obviously true and reveals she's trying to distinguish herself from the teenagers — while still acting like one.
jokes.explanation: The triple 'much' is a nice specificity that makes the line funnier than it would be with one. Lindsay's self-awareness in this moment (she IS much older) coexists with her total lack of self-awareness about her behavior. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Steve Holt: 'You don't look it.' [to Lindsay about her age] Lindsay: 'Oh, Steve. He thinks I'm young. You made my day again!'
jokes.setup: Lindsay fishes for a compliment about her age.
jokes.punchline: 'You made my day again!' — Lindsay mirroring what Steve said to Maeby earlier, revealing the competition is now explicit.
jokes.explanation: The callback to Steve's exact phrase ('made my day') from the earlier Maeby scene is efficient. Lindsay has appropriated her daughter's compliment — which is darker than it first appears. The delivery of 'again!' is the kicker.

jokes.text: George-Michael to Maeby about Lindsay: 'Wow, your Mom's pretty, uh, out there.' Maeby: 'She's not my mom.' Steve: 'But she said you were her daughter.' Maeby: 'His daughter. That's my dad.' Steve: 'That's a dude?' Maeby: 'And the worst part is he thinks he's passing.'
jokes.setup: Steve is confused about Lindsay's gender.
jokes.punchline: 'And the worst part is he thinks he's passing.' — Maeby's resigned kicker about Tobias's delusion.
jokes.explanation: The layered reveal (she's not my mom → that's my dad → he's a dude?) builds perfectly. But the real laugh is the button: 'he thinks he's passing.' The matter-of-fact resignation of Maeby's delivery — she's been living with this — is both funny and sad. High quotability. Maeby's weary expertise in her family's chaos is perfectly character-specific.

jokes.text: Gob helping Buster at the playground: 'I totally freaked out in front of that prosecutor today. Like a little girl. In a little dress. Little saddle shoes. Little pigtails.' Buster: 'Wow, that does sound like a little girl.'
jokes.setup: Gob is sharing his failure with Buster, using simile.
jokes.punchline: The simile escalates from 'little girl' to a full-costume description; Buster takes it literally and confirms the description is accurate.
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the self-description (adding dress, saddle shoes, pigtails) is funny as written; Buster's literal agreement ('Wow, that does sound like a little girl') is a perfect straight-man response that takes the metaphor at face value. Captures Buster's magnificent literal-mindedness.

jokes.text: Gob and Buster at the playground: Gob is clearly enjoying pushing Buster on the swings more than intended. Narrator: 'It was fun, but it wasn't making him a better soldier.' Buster: 'Push me higher!'
jokes.setup: The brothers are supposedly doing military training at the playground.
jokes.punchline: The narrator quietly notes the obvious — it's just playing — while Buster demands to go higher. The 'training' has become pure childhood regression.
jokes.explanation: The image of grown Bluth brothers at a playground being described as military training is funny. The narrator's deadpan observation ('wasn't making him a better soldier') is perfectly placed. Buster's regression to childhood is a consistent character note.

jokes.text: Oh, this is turning out to be much more fun than I'd hoped. [Gob, swinging on playground equipment while Buster tries to get motivated]
jokes.setup: Gob agreed to help motivate Buster, nominally as a favor.
jokes.punchline: Gob is having a wonderful time on the playground — the help is entirely for his own benefit.
jokes.explanation: Solid character beat — Gob's selfishness is always amusing when deployed with this level of guileless cheerfulness. Functional joke, not exceptional.

jokes.text: George Sr. holding broken device: 'Hey, by the way, I broke this thing. What the hell is it, anyway?' Michael: 'That's a breast pump, Dad.' George Sr.: 'Oh, well, I did not use it for that.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. has broken something in the attic that he doesn't recognize.
jokes.punchline: 'I did not use it for that.' — his defense implies familiarity with the device's purpose while denying its use, which raises more questions than it answers.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The structure is perfect: innocent question, devastating identification, then a defensive denial that creates a far worse implication than the original concern. 'I did not use it for that' is simultaneously reassuring and alarming — it suggests awareness of the alternative uses. The economy is excellent (three short sentences), and the line is immediately quotable. George Sr.'s self-incriminating defenses are a signature character trait. Earns its 8.5 because the structure is airtight and the implication is rich.

jokes.text: Wayne Jarvis: 'I was once called the worst audience participant Cirque du Soleil ever had.' Michael: 'This is a big accusation.' Jarvis: 'Well, Michael, I did not find their buffoonery amusing.'
jokes.setup: Jarvis is making a serious accusation about weapons of mass destruction.
jokes.punchline: He interrupts himself to share his Cirque du Soleil anecdote — a completely non-sequitur boast about his humorlessness, delivered with total seriousness.
jokes.explanation: Wayne Jarvis is established as a prosecutor of legendary humorlessness. 'Worst audience participant Cirque du Soleil ever had' is an extremely specific, absurd credential to drop mid-WMD briefing. The timing (interrupting himself during a serious accusation) and his defensive explanation ('I did not find their buffoonery amusing') are pitch-perfect. The character is defined entirely by this beat. 8.4 craft because the specificity and non-sequitur placement are brilliant character work.

jokes.text: Wayne Jarvis: 'He's guilty, Michael, of medium to heavy treason.'
jokes.setup: Jarvis is describing the severity of George Sr.'s crime.
jokes.punchline: 'Medium to heavy treason' — as if treason exists on a sliding scale that a prosecutor would casually calibrate.
jokes.explanation: Four words that do extraordinary work. 'Medium to heavy treason' applies a consumer-product weight-scale to the most serious crime imaginable, spoken by a humorless prosecutor who doesn't realize how absurd it sounds. The phrase creates its own taxonomy (implying 'light treason' exists as a category) and is delivered with complete professional sincerity. Near-perfect economy — this is the episode's most quotable line. Earns 8.7 for originality of phrasing and perfect character integration.

jokes.text: Lindsay claims Oscar disappeared 'because of something you said to him.' Michael: 'I can't talk right now.' Narrator: 'Really? What's wrong with your voice?' Michael: 'I can talk — sure.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay challenges Michael; Michael says he can't talk.
jokes.punchline: The narrator directly addresses Michael, mocking the phrase 'I can't talk' — a meta-moment where the narrator and Michael appear to briefly interact.
jokes.explanation: The narrator mocking Michael's phrasing ('Really? What's wrong with your voice?') is a fun meta-moment that also callbacks Lindsay's voice joke from earlier. The narrator rarely addresses characters directly, making this a notable structural deviation.

jokes.text: Lindsay tells Michael she ran into Steve Holt. Michael: 'Wow. Everything about you is so womanly. You're everything a woman should be.' Lindsay: 'Thank you, Steve.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay mentions her date with Steve Holt.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay mishears Michael's sarcasm as coming from Steve Holt — or simply isn't listening. Her 'Thank you, Steve' is both a misattribution and a revelation of her total fixation.
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two registers: Michael's sarcasm is unusually biting ('you're everything a woman should be' to his sister is bizarre), and Lindsay's total non-response to her brother and misattribution to Steve Holt is perfect character comedy. The miscommunication reveals how completely Steve Holt has replaced Michael in Lindsay's attention.

jokes.text: Michael's reaction after being presented with satellite photos of 'WMDs' — then discovering they're Tobias's body parts
jokes.setup: Jarvis shows Michael satellite photos that 'officials call definite proof of WMDs in Iraq' — hills, bunkers, a stream.
jokes.punchline: These are actually Tobias's accidentally self-photographed genitals, misidentified as landscape features and bunkers by forensic experts.
jokes.explanation: The episode's central structural joke — the entire WMD subplot, which drives military deployment and criminal prosecution, is resolved by Tobias's accidental nude selfie. The scale of the misdirection (from international geopolitics to genitals) is the joke, and the setup is beautifully maintained throughout the episode. Rewatch bonus because you can look back and see the Tobias phone-learning subplot was always building to this. Among the episode's most impressive structural achievements.

jokes.text: Michael: 'But Mom isn't the only one "finding love" where she least expects it — her words.' George Sr.: 'Whoa-whoa. "Finding love" — she said that? Where, here?' Michael: 'Yeah, she won't shut up about him. She said "tingling" like, 75 times.'
jokes.setup: Michael reports on Lucille's feelings for Oscar.
jokes.punchline: The specificity of '75 times' and 'tingling' — the word choice reveals Lucille in full romantic absurdity, and George Sr.'s horrified reaction is the payoff.
jokes.explanation: The '75 times' quantification is the best part — Michael's specificity implies he was counting, which is its own funny implication. 'Tingling' as Lucille's word of choice is appropriately unseemly. Functional scene-work.

jokes.text: Lucille on camera: '...gets me tingling. You know, with Oscar, there's love, and with this tingling, and with Oscar...' [Reaction: George Sr. listening in horror from the attic]
jokes.setup: Michael has told George Sr. that Lucille keeps saying 'tingling.'
jokes.punchline: We then hear Lucille actually say 'tingling' in context, confirming the absurdity — and George Sr.'s reaction (implicit in his subsequent flight) is the visual beat.
jokes.explanation: The cut to Lucille actually deploying 'tingling' is a satisfying payoff to the setup, but the scene's main comedic function is to precipitate George Sr.'s escape — the comedy is structural more than verbal.

jokes.text: Michael: 'This is going to sound like a strange question. Have you seen Dad?' Lucille: 'Of course not. And I'm not talking to you. You tried to scare away Oscar, which didn't work. He's back. I just, uh, I just want to have sex with you. That's, that's all I'm good for.' Michael: 'Did you hear that?' Narrator: 'He said he just wants to...' Michael: 'Good-bye, Mom.'
jokes.setup: Michael catches his mother mid-conversation with Oscar; her dialogue about being wanted only for sex is mid-complaint to Oscar.
jokes.punchline: Michael walks in at the worst moment, hears 'I just want to have sex with you,' tries to leave, and the narrator extends his discomfort by starting to repeat the line as Michael escapes.
jokes.explanation: The classic 'walked in at the worst moment' structure, executed well. Michael's horror and the narrator's sadistic timing of the repeat create a nice cringe beat. The visual of Michael's retreat while the narrator begins the sentence is a strong structural gag.

jokes.text: George Sr. (as Oscar) to Lucille: 'You smell like a pine cone.' Lucille: 'Yeah, that's the weed.' George Sr.: 'I went to my, uh, [bleep] trailer and, uh, I smoked some, like a cigarette.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. has disguised himself as Oscar to get close to Lucille. Lucille doesn't realize.
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s fragile cover is almost blown when he describes marijuana usage like someone who has never smoked (or never heard of the word 'marijuana') — bleeped and described as 'like a cigarette.'
jokes.explanation: The bleep on the marijuana word and the description 'like a cigarette' are funny because George Sr.'s disguise is paper-thin. His unfamiliarity with Oscar's stoner vocabulary is revealed in real time. Good character integration between the twins.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Should we take it into the bedroom?' Oscar/George Sr.: 'Only if you really love me.' Lucille: 'Of course I do.' [long pause] George Sr. then flees the bedroom.
jokes.setup: George Sr. is impersonating Oscar to test if Lucille loves him; she passes the test.
jokes.punchline: Having confirmed her love, the situation has now become... real. The long pause before he flees is the joke — he got what he came for but has now created a situation he absolutely cannot follow through on.
jokes.explanation: The pause IS the punchline — George Sr.'s plan has succeeded completely, and now he's trapped. The cringe of the awkward silence between 'Of course I do' and his flight is masterfully constructed. The structure is elegant: the setup takes the whole episode, the punchline is a beat of silence. Earns 8.1 for the economy of using silence as the gag and the character-specific irony (he wanted this, now he can't have it).

jokes.text: George Sr. runs from the bedroom: 'I'm on mushrooms! I'm on bad mushrooms!'
jokes.setup: George Sr. needs an excuse to flee the bedroom after confirming Lucille loves 'Oscar.'
jokes.punchline: 'I'm on mushrooms! I'm on bad mushrooms!' — a cover story that is simultaneously the most and least believable thing Oscar would say.
jokes.explanation: The escalation of 'bad mushrooms' is perfect — not just 'mushrooms' but specifically BAD ones. As a cover story it's desperate and inspired: Oscar actually would plausibly say this, making it barely credible as a cover. The urgency and repetition ('I'm on mushrooms! I'm on bad mushrooms!') makes it more absurd. Very quotable. Earns 8.1 for the character-specific logic of the excuse and the comedic escalation of the modifier.

jokes.text: News: 'We've obtained photographs that officials call definite proof of WMDs in Iraq.' / Cut to Buster at Army training. Sergeant: 'There's no time for that. We're shipping out without you. Didn't you see? Weapons of mass destruction?'
jokes.setup: The WMD satellite photos (actually Tobias's self-portrait) have gone viral and reached the military.
jokes.punchline: Buster's inability to climb a wall has been superseded by an international incident triggered by his uncle-in-law's camera phone. The cosmic scale of the misunderstanding is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The convergence of three separate plot threads (Tobias's phone, the WMD accusations, Buster's training wall) into one consequence is elegant plotting-as-comedy. The cause-and-effect chain (Tobias photographs himself → Patriot Act data sharing → global WMD alarm → Buster gets shipped out) is absurdist escalation across an entire episode. The structure earns it.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Those bastards.' Sergeant: 'I did not say that.' Buster: 'Are you going to let that slide? Are you going to allow your children and your children's children and any children that I might have out there to live in fear for the rest of their lives?!' / Sergeant: 'Climb that wall, homo! Climb it.'
jokes.setup: Buster is being motivated by WMDs to climb the training wall.
jokes.punchline: The sergeant's lawsuit-banned slur ('homo') returns — the threat of WMDs overrides his legal caution. The callback to the earlier 'lawsuit' joke pays off perfectly.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed callback payoff — the lawsuit-banned slur ('homo') returns exactly when sufficiently extreme motivation overrides the sergeant's legal caution. The structure plants the gag early, lets the episode breathe, and then pays it off with perfect comic logic: WMDs are the only thing strong enough to make him risk the lawsuit. Buster's 'children I might have out there' adds its own absurdist layer. Earns 8.2 for structural precision.

jokes.text: Climb that wall, homo! Climb it. [Gob, motivated by the WMD news to use the banned motivational slur]
jokes.setup: The sergeant can't use homophobic slurs to motivate Buster due to the lawsuit. The WMD crisis changes the stakes.
jokes.punchline: Gob, now filled with patriotic purpose, uses the banned phrase anyway — the national emergency apparently supersedes the lawsuit in his moral calculus.
jokes.explanation: Strong callback payoff. The joke from joke #7 was that the slur was banned due to a lawsuit; this callback pays it off by having Gob deploy it anyway out of WMD-motivated patriotism. The absurdity of national security overriding an HR ruling is perfectly AD. Buster actually climbs the wall, so the slur works — making Gob technically correct.

jokes.text: Gob and Buster at playground, Gob repeats: 'Now, when you do this without getting punched, you'll have more fun.' George-Michael arrives: 'They found WMDs in Iraq, so we got a half day.'
jokes.setup: The WMD news has created a school half-day; George-Michael arrives at the playground.
jokes.punchline: Gob repeats his earlier coaching line verbatim (callback), and the absurdity of children getting a half-day for WMDs is played as completely routine.
jokes.explanation: The exact repetition of Gob's coaching line is a clean callback gag. George-Michael's casual delivery of 'they found WMDs, so we got a half day' treats an international crisis as a school schedule matter, which is perfect teen-logic absurdism.

jokes.text: Gob to George-Michael after math test result: 'Well, the "B" got you glasses. Maybe this time you'll get a neck brace.'
jokes.setup: George-Michael reports a 'C' on his math test; Gob has context on the 'B' and glasses situation.
jokes.punchline: Gob suggests escalating consequence: each bad grade yields medical equipment. The implication is that 'C' warrants a neck brace.
jokes.explanation: A great extension of the glasses joke — now Gob (who knows none of the context) inadvertently proposes a logical progression: B→glasses, C→neck brace. The escalating medical equipment scale is darkly funny, and Gob's delivery of this as practical advice is perfectly character-specific.

jokes.text: George-Michael to Gob about Lindsay: 'She's on her way to meet Steve Holt.' George-Michael: 'I do; I'm crazy about him. But he's obsessed with her. That's all he wants to talk about.' / Narrator: 'Lindsay felt bad... But she would have felt worse if she'd remained even a moment longer. But it's only because he thinks she's got a penis. Oh, I told him she was a tranny.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay feels guilty about competing with her daughter for Steve Holt's attention.
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals Maeby has solved the problem by telling Steve that Lindsay is transgender — the casual delivery of 'Oh, I told him she was a tranny' as a solution is both absurd and character-perfect for Maeby.
jokes.explanation: The reveal structure is excellent: we're told Lindsay 'felt bad,' given a false bottom (she would feel worse if she'd stayed), then the actual resolution is revealed — Maeby weaponized Tobias's earlier gender confusion as a practical solution. The callback to the 'that's a dude?' scene makes this land harder. Maeby's problem-solving ethics are perfectly captured in 'Oh, I told him she was a tranny.'

jokes.text: But it's only because he thinks she's got a penis. / Oh, I told him she was a tranny.
jokes.setup: Lindsay feels bad that Steve is obsessed with her and not Maeby. Maeby reveals Steve is obsessed because he thinks Lindsay has a penis.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay cheerfully confirms: 'Oh, I told him she was a tranny' — revealing that Lindsay deliberately told Steve she's transgender, presumably to (a) explain away her age, (b) be provocative, or (c) out of pure self-serving chaos.
jokes.explanation: A satisfying reveal that recontextualizes everything in the Lindsay/Steve subplot. Lindsay's breezy 'Oh, I told him she was a tranny' is delivered like it's the most natural solution to a problem. The callback structure (we get the implication in joke #46, the Steve compliments in #52, and now the explanation) is tight. Lindsay using Tobias's gender presentation as a personal strategy is peak Bluth selfishness.

jokes.text: Michael to George Sr.: 'You deserted her, Dad. You know, you escaped, and then you faked your death. I'm not so sure she sees you as that reliable.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. complains that Lucille deserted him.
jokes.punchline: Michael's clinical summary of George Sr.'s crimes against the marriage ('escaped, faked your death') as reasons she might not see him as 'that reliable' is a masterpiece of understatement.
jokes.explanation: 'Not that reliable' as the conclusion after listing 'escaped and faked your death' is a great understatement. The mundane phrasing deflates the dramatic events to ordinary relationship problems. Michael's matter-of-fact delivery is character-specific.

jokes.text: Wayne Jarvis: 'Michael, when we started talking to you, we didn't have anything. But now... we got something. And you're going to do time for it.' Michael: 'Those are the pictures?' Barry: 'They're all over the news.' Michael: 'Those are balls.'
jokes.setup: Jarvis is threatening Michael with photographic evidence of WMDs. Michael looks at the photos.
jokes.punchline: 'Those are balls.' — the WMD photos are revealed to be Tobias's accidental self-portrait. The solemn buildup to the revelation is undercut by the bluntest possible identification.
jokes.explanation: The episode's structural climax and its biggest laugh. The entire WMD subplot — satellite photos, Patriot Act data sharing, military deployment — collapses into three words: 'Those are balls.' The economy is extraordinary. The setup is the entire episode. The reveal connects to the episode title ('Sad Sack'). Barry's straight-faced 'This close, they always look like landscape' adds a perfectly deadpan second beat. This is the episode's best joke by structure and impact — the punchline to a 17-minute setup.

jokes.text: Barry: 'This close, they always look like landscape.' / Michael: 'Nope, you're looking at balls.'
jokes.setup: Barry tries to defend the misidentification of the photos.
jokes.punchline: Michael dismisses Barry's hedge with total authority: 'Nope, you're looking at balls.'
jokes.explanation: The perfect second-beat to the 'those are balls' revelation. Barry's attempted rationalization ('This close, they always look like landscape') sets up Michael's flat dismissal. 'Nope, you're looking at balls' is delivered with zero equivocation, making it funnier than almost any longer response could be. High quotability.

jokes.text: Forensic scientists, generals, government officials all reviewing the photos: 'They're balls? We're looking at balls. Let's turn it around.' / 'Copy on the balls.'
jokes.setup: The WMD photos are being assessed by multiple branches of government.
jokes.punchline: Each successive level of authority confirms 'balls' with increasing professionalism, including military radio protocol: 'Copy on the balls.'
jokes.explanation: 'Copy on the balls.' is one of the great lines of the episode — the military radio protocol applied to a testicle identification is perfect escalation. The show turns bureaucratic language into comedy by making it the medium for absurd content. The montage of government officials all confirming 'balls' is a peak Arrested Development sequence: absurdist escalation with institutional satire.

jokes.text: News anchor: 'We got one wrong, and we do apologize.' / Tobias (watching TV): 'I'm on TV.'
jokes.setup: The news issues a correction — the WMDs were a misidentification.
jokes.punchline: Tobias's response to news of his own accidental photograph — 'I'm on TV' — reveals that his only takeaway from an international incident is that he achieved his dream of media exposure.
jokes.explanation: Two words that encapsulate Tobias's entire character. While the news corrects a false WMD alarm that caused international panic and military mobilization, Tobias's response is 'I'm on TV' — his dream realized in the worst possible context. The gap between the gravity of the situation and Tobias's reaction is enormous, and his complete narcissism in that gap is the joke. Near-flawless character integration. Among the episode's best lines for character economy.

jokes.text: Got the glasses. / Now you'll do better on those math exams. / I think I blew the eye exam too, because every time I take these off, I feel better.
jokes.setup: George-Michael has received his glasses — the whole plan was to keep him away from Ann by giving him an eye exam.
jokes.punchline: George-Michael has correctly identified that the glasses make him feel worse — his 'vision problem' was nonexistent, and the lenses are presumably wrong. The whole scheme has backfired.
jokes.explanation: Good callback payoff — the eye exam subplot is resolved with George-Michael accidentally confirming he never needed glasses. Michael's scheme is quietly undone.

jokes.text: George-Michael gets glasses: 'Got the glasses. Now you'll do better on those math exams.' George-Michael: 'I think I blew the eye exam too, because every time I take these off, I feel better.'
jokes.setup: Michael triumphantly presents the glasses he manipulated George-Michael into getting.
jokes.punchline: George-Michael has failed the eye exam on purpose (as we saw) — his glasses make his vision worse. The callback confirms he protected Ann at the cost of his own eyesight.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the glasses running gag is satisfying and character-revealing. George-Michael's passive subversion of Michael's manipulation (failing the eye exam intentionally) confirms his character — loyal to Ann even at cost to himself. 'Every time I take these off, I feel better' is a perfectly understated way to reveal the deception.

jokes.text: Michael looks at George-Michael's glasses: 'Hey, I've seen this before.' Narrator: 'For a while, George-Michael had shared a room with his Uncle Tobias.' [Flashback or implication that these are Tobias's glasses]
jokes.setup: Michael recognizes the glasses frames.
jokes.punchline: They're Tobias's glasses — George-Michael took them from when they shared a room. The eye exam was not just intentionally failed; the glasses were never even prescribed for him.
jokes.explanation: A brilliant structural payoff that retroactively reframes everything. Not only did GM fail the eye exam intentionally, he was also wearing Tobias's prescription glasses all along — meaning the entire glasses subplot was based on a fake need, addressed with a fake exam, resulting in glasses he borrowed anyway. The layers of deception (on Michael, on the doctor, on himself) make this rich. High rewatch bonus as the callback is most satisfying when you trace all the threads.

jokes.text: George-Michael's reaction to Michael's realization about Tobias's glasses. GM says 'Uh, there's a ladder.' Tobias's voice: 'Oh, I don't need it.' [Tobias has climbed into the attic to retrieve his glasses]
jokes.setup: Michael has identified the glasses as Tobias's.
jokes.punchline: Tobias has apparently climbed up to retrieve them — without the ladder. The logistics of Tobias in the attic are left completely unexplained, which is funnier.
jokes.explanation: The joke is that Tobias is in the attic without explanation, and his refusal of the ladder implies he climbed up some other way — never explained, which is funnier than any explanation would be. The absurdism of not addressing the logistics is a show signature.

jokes.text: George Sr. in attic: 'You know, you deserted her, Dad... I don't know whether it's just Tracy's maternity clothes, but it's very nice to see a softer side of you.'
jokes.setup: Michael shares a tender moment with his father, who is still wearing his deceased mother's maternity clothes.
jokes.punchline: The 'softer side' is literally the maternity clothes — the joke works on both an emotional and visual level simultaneously.
jokes.explanation: The maternity clothes callback pays off emotionally and comedically in the same beat. 'Softer side' is both literal (the soft fabric of maternity wear) and figurative (George Sr.'s unusual vulnerability). The double meaning lands because the show trusts the audience to hold both readings simultaneously.

jokes.text: George Sr. smells Michael's clothing: 'You found her perfume, huh?' Michael: 'There was just a little left in the bottle. That was the end of it.' George Sr.: 'It's nice on you.'
jokes.setup: The episode's emotional peak — George Sr. smells his late wife's perfume on Michael.
jokes.punchline: 'It's nice on you.' — a compliment that is both genuinely tender and quietly absurd (the deceased wife's perfume on her son, pronounced nice).
jokes.explanation: The show at its best: comedy and genuine emotion occupying the same beat. The situation is absurd (fugitive patriarch in maternity clothes, sniffing his son for his dead wife's scent), but the delivery makes it almost sweet. 'It's nice on you.' earns its laughs through discomfort and tenderness simultaneously. One of the more complex emotional beats in the episode.

jokes.text: On the Next Arrested Development — Oscar returns and is immediately cold-shouldered by Lucille. Narrator: '...which was what attracted him to her in the first place.'
jokes.setup: Oscar returns after going distant; Lucille reverts to her cold self.
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals Oscar is attracted to Lucille's cruelty — her affection was repellent; her coldness is what he loves. The cycle is complete.
jokes.explanation: The 'On the Next' segment closes the Lucille/Oscar loop perfectly. Oscar's attraction to cruelty is revealed in the callback to the opening — the episode began with him fleeing kindness and ends with him returning for cruelty. The circular logic is both funny and character-defining.

jokes.text: On the Next — Maeby gives Lindsay a gift; Lindsay is touched. Maeby: 'I was hoping we could go to dinner tonight.' Lindsay: 'Oh, I thought you were going to go see Steve Holt at school.' Maeby: 'No, I just wanted to check him out and make sure he's good enough for my daughter.' Lindsay: 'Is it...?' Maeby: 'It's a "Sheh-ma-lee." It's gorgeous. I'm wearing it to dinner.'
jokes.setup: Maeby reverses her stance — now she's checking Steve Holt out 'for her daughter' (Lindsay).
jokes.punchline: Maeby mirrors Lindsay's earlier inappropriate behavior, calling her own mother 'my daughter' and inspecting her romantic prospects. The gift name 'Sheh-ma-lee' (presumably 'she-male') is an appalling final pun.
jokes.explanation: Maeby calling Lindsay 'my daughter' completes the role reversal established throughout the episode. The 'Sheh-ma-lee' gift name ('she-male') continues the Tobias gender-presentation thread from the episode. The structural inversion (daughter now parenting the mother) is character-specific to Maeby's unique family situation.

jokes.text: On the Next — Tobias, 'traumatized by the public exposure, returns to an unfortunate quirk.' Tobias: 'Thank God — my cutoffs!' [finds denim cutoffs in attic] George Sr.: 'There were cutoffs up here?'
jokes.setup: Tobias's accidental nude photo has gone internationally viral. He's described as 'traumatized by the public exposure.'
jokes.punchline: His response to trauma is finding his Daisy Duke cutoffs — the infamous running gag garment. And George Sr.'s 'There were cutoffs up here?' reveals he's been sharing the attic with Tobias's clothing.
jokes.explanation: The never-nude gag returns as a perfect episode capper. 'Public exposure' is the setup (Tobias was literally photographed and broadcast globally) and his response is to find more coverage — his cutoffs. The wordplay on 'exposure' is elegant. George Sr.'s discovery that Tobias's wardrobe has been in the attic all along is a nice grace note. Strong character integration for a series running gag.

tmdb_overview: George Sr. fears he's losing Lucille to Oscar. Gob motivates Buster for the Army while Lindsay spends more time with Maeby to get closer to Steve Holt and Tobias starts working out.
insight: Character escalation drives 71 jokes across 44 minutes, sustaining Season 2's comedic momentum.
